# Avoiding Default | Federal Student Aid#1

There are steps you can take to repay your federal student loan successfully and avoid going into default. Understanding your loan agreement , staying on top of your loan information , and making sure to contact your loan servicer if you are having trouble making payments can help you avoid default. If you don t make a payment on time or if you miss making a payment , your loan is delinquent and late fees may be assessed. Even if you are delinquent on your loan, you may still be able to avoid default , so it s important that you contact your loan servicer immediately. Take Steps to Avoid Default Avoid default. Take the time to fully understand your loan agreement and the types of loans you are receiving. It s also important that you not borrow more than you need or more than you expect to be able to repay. Develop a sound and realistic financial plan. If your federal student loan is delinquent , check out the Student Loan Debt Collection Assistant, which the U.S. Department of Education developed in partnership with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. The tool provides information about how to access the full range of special repayment options available to you. Understand Your Loan and Loan Agreement Understand the type of aid you are receiving. Loans, unlike most grants, scholarships, or work - study funds, must be repaid. Know the type of loan you are receiving. Understand the costs of getting the loan, the interest rate, and the repayment terms for the loan. Read Know Before You Owe from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. Read your promissory note. It is a legal document. Signing a promissory note means you agree to repay the loan according to the terms of the note. You must repay all the loans you receive, even if you don t complete your education. Manage Your Borrowing Borrow only what you need to pay for your college expenses. Create a budget to determine how much you really need to borrow. Contact your school s financial aid office to request a lower loan amount instead of borrowing the maximum amount you might be eligible to receive. Complete Financial Awareness Counseling. Track Your Loans Online Find information about all of your federal student loans from the U.S. Department of Education by logging in to " My Federal Student Aid . " Keep Good Records Keep the following important documents in an organized file : Financial aid offers Loan counseling materials entrance counseling and exit counseling Your promissory note(s) Amount of all student loans you borrow Account number for each student loan you receive Loan servicer contact information Loan disclosure(s) Payment schedules Record of your monthly payments Notes about any questions you ask about your student loan, the answers, and the name of the person to whom you spoke Deferment or forbearance paperwork and notes of any phone calls to the loan servicer that you paid your loan in full Documentation Notify Your Loan Servicer Tell your loan servicer when you need help making your monthly payments ; graduate; withdraw from school; drop below half - time enrollment status at school ; change your name, address, or Social Security number ; transfer to another school ; or experience a change in your life that might impact your loan payments. What if I can t make my monthly payment? If you are having trouble making your monthly payments , contact your loan servicer immediately. Your loan servicer can help you understand your options. You may be able to switch repayment plans to get a lower monthly payment , consider an income - driven repayment plan , change your payment due date , or get a deferment or forbearance. NEVER ignore delinquency or default notices from your loan servicer. Consider Simplifying Repayment with Consolidation You might wish to combine your multiple federal student loans into a single Direct Consolidation Loan to simplify repayment. There may be tradeoffs, so understand the advantages and possible disadvantages of consolidation before you apply.

# Benefits Planner: Survivors | How You Apply | Social Security Administration#1

Benefits Planner: Survivors | How You Apply We should be notified immediately when a person dies. However , you cannot report a death or apply for survivors benefits online. If you need to report a death or apply for benefits , call 1 - 800 - 772 - 1213 TTY 1 - 800 - 325 - 0778. You can speak to a Social Security representative between 7 a.m. and 7 p.m. Monday through Friday. You can also visit your local Social Security office. An appointment is not required , but if you call ahead and schedule one, it may reduce the time you spend waiting to speak to someone. When You Report a Death If you are getting benefits on your spouse's or parent's record : You generally will not need to file an application for survivors benefits. We'll automatically change any monthly benefits you receive to survivors benefits after we receive the report of death. We may be able to pay the Special Lump - Sum Death Payment automatically. If you are getting retirement or disability benefits on your own record : You will need to apply for the survivors benefits. We will check to see whether you can get a higher benefit as a widow or widower. If you are not getting benefits , you should apply for survivors benefits promptly because, in some cases, benefits may not be retroactive. Documents Needed to Apply Please select the benefit you will be applying for from the list below to see what information and documents you may need when you apply : Widows / Widowers or Surviving Divorced Spouse's Benefits Child's Benefits Mother's or Father's Benefits You must have a child under age 16 or disabled in your care. Lump - Sum Death Payment Parent's Benefits You must have been dependent on your child at the time of his or her death. If you don't have all the documents you need , don't delay applying for Social Security. In many cases , your local Social Security office can contact your state Bureau of Vital Statistics and verify your information online at no cost to you. If we can't verify your information online , we can still help you get the information you need.

# Regional Cuisine and Recipes

Heritage Grains and Legumes: A Revival in Regional Cooking In the past decade, there has been a burgeoning interest in the farm-to-table movement, where chefs and home cooks alike seek to reconnect with the culinary roots of their regions. This has led to a resurrection of heritage grains and legumes - crops that have been overshadowed by commercial farming practices but are finally stepping back into the spotlight. These ancient varieties are not just a nod to traditional diets; they're an exploration of flavors, textures, and nutritional benefits that have been largely forgotten. Uncovering the Wealth of Heirloom Varieties On a crisp September morning in 2023, the bustling lanes of the Springfield Farmers' Market were abuzz with a new trend. Local farmers proudly showcased their varieties of heritage grains like einkorn, emmer, and spelt, alongside heirloom legumes such as Jacob's Cattle beans and Christmas limas. These were not the usual suspects found in the grocery aisles but were greeted with curiosity and excitement by food enthusiasts. Farmers like Sarah Gilbert and James O'Neill are at the forefront of this agricultural revival. Gilbert, a third-generation farmer, embraced the heritage grains project in early 2024 after attending an educational workshop on sustainable farming practices. "It's not just about growing crops," she explains. "It's about preserving a legacy and offering something that has a story, a history." The Nutritional and Environmental Edge The advantages of heritage grains and legumes extend beyond their rich histories. Nutritionists like Dr. Richard Hammond have long advocated for the diversity these crops bring to the table. "Heritage grains possess a complexity of flavors and textures, but even more importantly, they're nutritional powerhouses," Hammond says, pointing out the higher levels of protein, fiber, and essential minerals these grains often contain compared to their modern counterparts. Moreover, these crops are usually more adaptable to local soil and weather conditions, making them resilient in the face of climate change. Their deep-rooted nature also helps to improve soil health – a critical component of sustainable agriculture. A Taste of the Past for a Sustainable Future To integrate these old-world crops into modern meals, chefs like Emily Thompson are taking up the challenge. Since the fall of 2023, Thompson has dedicated herself to showcasing heritage grains and legumes on her restaurant's menu. "It's about re-educating our palates and playing with flavors we've overlooked for too long," she says. Her signature dishes include a hearty farro risotto enriched with wild mushrooms and a rustic lentil stew that spotlights the earthy French du Puy lentils. Not only do these dishes offer a fresh take on traditional recipes, but they also support local farmers and encourage sustainable farming practices. Cultivating Community Through Gastronomy The revival of these crops has also spurred a sense of community among those who value food sovereignty and provenance. Food festivals dedicated to heritage grains and legumes, like the "Grain to Glory Symposium" held in October 2024, have become meeting grounds for farmers, chefs, and consumers passionate about the movement. At such events, participants can engage in workshops that explore everything from planting and harvesting to cooking and baking with these revived crops. Here, they can exchange seeds, recipes, and experiences, building a shared culinary heritage that's deeply rooted in their region. Teaching Future Generations Educators have recognized the invaluable lessons that can be learned from heritage grains and legumes. In schools across the region, gardening programs have incorporated the cultivation of these crops into their syllabus. Children are getting hands-on experience sowing, tending, and harvesting, which not only teaches them about agriculture but also encourages them to try unfamiliar foods. Local high school teacher Mark Donnelly incorporated a heritage crops project into his biology class curriculum in September 2025. "When students connect with where their food comes from, they develop a deeper respect for the environment and the people who grow their food," Donnelly states. The Farmer's Role in the Revival Farmers have been pivotal in reviving these ancient varieties. They are not just growers; they are stewards of biodiversity. By cultivating heritage grains and legumes, they maintain the genetic diversity of our crops, which is crucial for food security. These farmers often work in close collaboration with seed banks and agricultural institutions to ensure the preservation and propagation of rare seeds. By taking a vested interest in the past, they're planting the seeds for a more diverse and resilient future. Bridging Culinary Art and Sustainable Practices Within the culinary world, the resurrection of heritage grains and legumes is a call to redesign menus and transform kitchens. Chefs have become pivotal figures in this movement as they create dishes that tell the story of the region and its agricultural heritage. Their creativity has been central to winning over the palates of patrons who may be more accustomed to processed and mass-produced foods. The Harvest of Tomorrow Begins Today As this trend continues to grow, it serves as a reminder that what we choose to grow, cook, and eat has a profound impact not only on our health but also on our culture and environment. Heritage grains and legumes represent a bridge between our ancestral past and our sustainable future, offering a journey of culinary discovery for those who are willing to embrace the grains of yesteryear. Through education, community engagement, and a dedication to biodiversity, this revival is more than just a trend; it's a renaissance that opens the door to rediscovering the wealth of flavors and nutrition that regional cooking has to offer. The story of these heirloom crops is still being written, with every season bringing new chapters of growth and gastronomic innovation. As we continue to explore and value the role that these grains and legumes play in our diets and ecosystems, the revival of heritage crops is sure to become a staple of regional cooking for generations to come.

# Local Economy and Market

Navigating Regulatory and Compliance Issues: Small Business Struggles Starting a small business can be an incredibly rewarding venture, but it also comes with its fair share of challenges. Among the most daunting are the regulatory and compliance issues that can vary significantly from one jurisdiction to another. After September 1, 2023, several small business owners have voiced their concerns about the increasing complexity of these regulations. To gain deeper insight into these challenges, we sat down with Elizabeth Harmon, a compliance expert with over 20 years of experience in the field, on October 15, 2023. Her perspectives shed light on common pitfalls for small business owners and offered pragmatic advice for navigating the complex regulatory landscape. Understanding the Regulatory Maze Small business owners often find themselves overwhelmed by the sheer number of regulations that apply to their operations. According to Elizabeth, it starts with recognizing that compliance isn't a one-size-fits-all scenario. "Every industry has its unique set of regulations, and within those industries, the requirements can differ based on location, the size of the business, and even the products or services it offers," she explained. For instance, a restaurant will have different health and safety regulations compared to a construction business. She emphasized the importance of doing thorough research or consulting with experts to understand the specific regulations that apply to your business. "Ignorance of the law is not a defense," Elizabeth cautioned, "and the consequences of non-compliance can be severe, ranging from fines to the closure of your business." The Compliance Lifecycle Our conversation highlighted that compliance is not a static task—it's a continuous process. Elizabeth referred to this as the "compliance lifecycle," which she breaks down into several essential stages: **Awareness:** Small business owners need to be aware of both existing regulations and any legislative changes that may affect their operations. Elizabeth mentioned that since November 2023, there has been a surge in environmental regulations affecting local businesses, emphasizing the speed at which the regulatory landscape can change. **Implementation:** Once aware, businesses must implement policies, procedures, and perhaps even structural changes to comply with these regulations. This step often requires investment in training, infrastructure, or consulting with experts. **Monitoring:** Compliance requires ongoing monitoring to ensure continued adherence to regulations. Elizabeth highlights a common pitfall here: "Businesses often think that once they have complied, that's it. But regulatory bodies update rules regularly, and new requirements can come into play." **Reporting:** Many regulatory bodies require periodic reporting. Elizabeth noted, "Keeping accurate and timely records is crucial for smooth reporting. Slips in this area can lead to penalties or, worse, audits." **Improvement:** Regulations often aim to improve areas of safety, quality, or ethics within industry practices. Elizabeth advises businesses to see compliance not just as a legal requirement but as an opportunity to enhance their operations. Financial Implications of Compliance Another significant issue small business owners face is the financial burden of compliance. Elizabeth remarked, "Compliance can be expensive, especially for smaller entities." From updating equipment to meet safety standards to paying for necessary permits and licenses, the costs can quickly add up. We discussed how these financial pressures had led to the closure of several small businesses in the last quarter of 2023. To help mitigate these risks, Elizabeth suggests looking into government grants or local support programs designed to assist small businesses with compliance-related expenses. Technological Tools and Compliance The conversation then shifted to the role of technology in aiding compliance. "There's a multitude of software tools designed to help small businesses stay compliant," Elizabeth said. She pointed out that while there is an upfront cost, investing in compliance software can streamline the process and reduce the risk of human error. She highlighted that modern compliance software can automatically update changes in regulations, which is particularly valuable given the frequency of legislative updates. Moreover, they can ease the reporting process and help you maintain foolproof documentation should an audit occur. Building a Compliance-Friendly Culture One of the key takeaways from our interview with Elizabeth was the importance of fostering a compliance-friendly culture within the business. "Compliance should be everyone's responsibility," she urged. By educating employees on the importance of regulations and integrating compliance into the very fabric of your business model, it becomes less of a daunting task and more of a standard operating procedure. The Human Aspect of Compliance Despite all the tools and processes in place, Elizabeth stressed that the human aspect should not be overlooked. Situations can vary widely and having a designated person or team to handle compliance can provide the nuance and flexibility that automated systems may lack. "Having someone with a firm handle on the specifics of your business's compliance needs is invaluable," Elizabeth said. She also recommended building relationships with the local business community, including joining chambers of commerce or industry associations, which can act as great resources for information and support. Looking Forward As we wrapped up our comprehensive discussion, it became clear that navigating regulatory and compliance issues is an evolving journey for small businesses. The key lies in remaining vigilant, informed, and proactive. While the challenges can be substantial, Elizabeth's insights highlighted the critical role that understanding, planning, and adopting a culture of compliance plays in the success and sustainability of small businesses moving forward. With the shared wisdom and practical advice offered by experts like Elizabeth, small business owners can learn to not only manage these challenges but turn them into opportunities for growth and improvement.

# Benefits Planner: Survivors | If You Are The Survivor | Social Security Administration#2

Just as you plan for your family's protection if you die , you should consider the Social Security benefits that may be available if you are the survivor that is, the spouse, child, or parent of a worker who dies. That person must have worked long enough under Social Security to qualify for benefits. How Your Spouse Earns Social Security Survivors Benefits A worker can earn up to four credits each year. In 2019 , for example , your spouse can earn one credit for each $1,360 of wages or self - employment income. When your spouse has earned $5,440 , they have earned their four credits for the year. The number of credits needed to provide benefits for survivors depends on the worker's age when they die. No one needs more than 40 credits 10 years of work to be eligible for any Social Security benefit. But , the younger a person is , the fewer credits they must have for family members to receive survivors benefits. Benefits can be paid to the worker's children and the surviving spouse who is caring for the children even if the worker doesn't have the required number of credits. They can get benefits if the worker has credit for one and one - half years of work 6 credits in the three years just before their death. Each person s situation is different and you need to talk to a Social Security claims representative about your choices. When A Family Member Dies We should be notified as soon as possible when a person dies. However , you cannot report a death or apply for survivors benefits online. If you need to report a death or apply for benefits , call 1 - 800 - 772 - 1213 TTY 1 - 800 - 325 - 0778. You can speak to a Social Security representative between 7 a.m. and 7 p.m. Monday through Friday. You can also visit your local Social Security office. An appointment is not required , but if you call ahead and schedule one, it may reduce the time you spend waiting to speak to someone. Does Social Security pay death benefits? A one - time payment of $255 can be paid to the surviving spouse if he or she was living with the deceased; or, if living apart, was receiving certain Social Security benefits on the deceased s record. If there is no surviving spouse , the payment is made to a child who is eligible for benefits on the deceased s record in the month of death. What happens if the deceased received monthly benefits? If the deceased was receiving Social Security benefits , you must return the benefit received for the month of death and any later months. For example , if the person died in July , you must return the benefits paid in August. How you return the benefits depends on how the deceased received benefits : For funds received by direct deposit , contact the bank or other financial institution. Request that any funds received for the month of death or later be returned to Social Security. Benefits received by check must be returned to Social Security as soon as possible. Do not cash any checks received for the month in which the person dies or later. Who receives benefits? Certain family members may be eligible to receive monthly benefits, including : A widow or widower age 60 or older age 50 or older if disabled ; A surviving divorced spouse , under certain circumstances ; A widow or widower at any age who is caring for the deceased s child who is under age 16 or disabled and receiving benefits on their record ; An unmarried child of the deceased who is : Younger than age 18 or up to age 19 if he or she is a full - time student in an elementary or secondary school ; or Age 18 or older with a disability that began before age 22. Are other family members eligible? Under certain circumstances , the following family members may be eligible : A stepchild, grandchild, step grandchild, or adopted child ; and Parents , age 62 or older , who were dependent on the deceased for at least half of their support. Widow Or Widower If you are the widow or widower of a person who worked long enough under Social Security , you can : receive full benefits at full retirement age for survivors or reduced benefits as early as age 60. begin receiving benefits as early as age 50 if you are disabled and the disability started before or within seven years of the worker's death. receive survivors benefits at any age , if you have not remarried and you take care of the deceased worker's child who is under age 16 or is disabled and receives benefits on the worker's record. If you remarry after you reach age 60 age 50 if disabled , your remarriage will not affect your eligibility for survivors benefits. A widow, widower, or surviving divorced spouse cannot apply online for survivors benefits. You should contact Social Security at 1 - 800 - 772 - 1213 to request an appointment. If you are deaf or hard of hearing, call our TTY number at 1 - 800 - 325 - 0778. If you wish to apply for disability benefits as a survivor , you can speed up the disability application process if you complete an Adult Disability Report and have it available at the time of your appointment. We use the same definition of disability for widows and widowers as we do for workers. A few other situations: If you already receive benefits as a spouse , your benefit will automatically convert to survivors benefits after we receive the report of death. If you are also eligible for retirement benefits but haven't applied yet , you have an additional option. You can apply for retirement or survivors benefits now and switch to the other higher benefit at a later date. For those already receiving retirement benefits , you can only apply for benefits as a widow or widower if the retirement benefit you receive is less than the benefits you would receive as a survivor. Surviving Divorced Spouse If you are the divorced spouse of a worker who dies , you could get benefits the same as a widow or widower, provided that your marriage lasted 10 years or more. Benefits paid to you as a surviving divorced spouse won't affect the benefit amount for other survivors getting benefits on the worker's record. If you remarry after you reach age 60 age 50 if disabled , the remarriage will not affect your eligibility for survivors benefits. If you are caring for a child under age 16 or disabled and gets benefits on the record of your former spouse , you would not have to meet the length - of - marriage rule. The child must be your former spouse's natural or legally adopted child. Minor Or Disabled Child If you are the unmarried child under 18 up to age 19 if attending elementary or secondary school full time of a worker who dies , you can be eligible to receive Social Security survivors benefits. And you can get benefits at any age if you were disabled before age 22 and remain disabled. Besides the worker's natural children , their stepchildren, grandchildren, or adopted children may receive benefits under certain circumstances. For Your Parents If you are the dependent parent , who is at least age 62 , of a worker who dies , you may be eligible to receive Social Security survivors benefits. You must have been receiving at least half of your support from your working child and you must not be eligible to receive a retirement benefit that is higher than the benefit we could pay on your child s record. Generally, you must not have married after your deceased adult child s death ; however, there are some exceptions. Besides being the natural parent , you could also be the stepparent, or the adoptive parent if you became the deceased worker s parent before he or she was age 16. Survivors Benefit Amount Your survivors benefit amount is based on the earnings of the person who died. The more they paid into Social Security , the higher your benefits would be. The monthly amount you would get is a percentage of the deceased's basic Social Security benefit. It depends on your age and the type of benefit you are eligible to receive. If the person who died was receiving reduced benefits , we base your survivors benefit on that amount. These are examples of the benefits that survivors may receive : Widow or widower, full retirement age or older 100 percent of the deceased worker's benefit amount ; Widow or widower , age 60 full retirement age 71 to 99 percent of the deceased worker's basic amount ; Disabled widow or widower aged 50 through 59 71 percent ; Widow or widower , any age, caring for a child under age 16 75 percent ; A child under age 18 19 if still in elementary or secondary school or disabled 75 percent ; and Dependent parent of the deceased worker, age 62 or older : One surviving parent 82 percent. Two surviving parents 75 percent to each parent. Percentages for a surviving divorced spouse would be the same as above. There may also be a special lump - sum death benefit. Maximum Family Amount There's a limit to the amount that family members can receive each month. The limit varies , but it is generally equal to between 150 and 180 percent of the basic benefit rate. If the sum of the benefits payable to family members is greater than this limit , the benefits will be reduced proportionately. Any benefits paid to a surviving divorced spouse based on disability or age won't count toward this maximum amount. Other Things You Need To Know There are limits on how much survivors may earn while they receive benefits. Benefits for a widow, widower, or surviving divorced spouse may be affected by several additional factors : If you remarry before you reach age 60 age 50 if disabled , you cannot receive benefits as a surviving spouse while you are married. If you remarry after you reach age 60 age 50 if disabled , you will continue to qualify for benefits on your deceased spouse's Social Security record. If you receive benefits as a widow, widower, or surviving divorced spouse , you can switch to your own retirement benefit as early as age 62. This assumes you are eligible for retirement benefits and your retirement rate is higher than your rate as a widow, widower, or surviving divorced spouse. In many cases , a widow or widower can begin receiving one benefit at a reduced rate and then, at full retirement age, switch to the other benefit at an unreduced rate. If you will also receive a pension based on work not covered by Social Security, such as government or foreign work , your Social Security benefits as a survivor may be affected. A Special Lump-Sum Death Payment A surviving spouse or child may receive a special lump - sum death payment of $255 if they meet certain requirements. Generally , the lump - sum is paid to the surviving spouse who was living in the same household as the worker when they died. If they were living apart , the surviving spouse can still receive the lump - sum if, during the month the worker died, they : were already receiving benefits on the worker's record or became eligible for benefits upon the worker's death. If there's no eligible surviving spouse , the lump - sum can be paid to the worker's child or children if, during the month the worker died, the child : was already receiving benefits on the worker's record or became eligible for benefits upon the worker's death. If the eligible surviving spouse or child is not currently receiving benefits , they must apply for this payment within two years of the date of death. For more information about this lump - sum payment , contact your local Social Security office or call 1 - 800 - 772 - 1213 1 - 800 - 325 - 0778.

# Board hearings with a Veterans Law Judge | Veterans Affairs#1

Board hearings with a Veterans Law Judge The process for Board hearings has changed If you're appealing a VA decision under the new process , keep reading below to learn how to request and prepare for a hearing. You'll follow the new process if you're : Appealing a VA decision you received on or after February 19 , 2019 Participating in the Rapid Appeals Modernization Program Opting into the new process using VA Form 10182 If you re using VA Form 9 to appeal a VA decision you received before February 19 , 2019,go to the Board of Veterans' Appeals BVA Hearing page Why should I consider requesting a Board hearing? You can request a video conference hearing if you want to provide testimony to a judge about your appeal. The hearing will be transcribed and added to your appeal file. Am I required to have a Board hearing? You aren t required to have a hearing. A Board hearing is always optional. Learn more about Board hearings Request a Board hearing Prepare for your Board hearing Find out what to expect at your Board hearing Reschedule or withdraw your Board hearing request Request a Board hearing How do I request a Board hearing? When you fill out the Decision Review Request: Board Appeal VA Form 10182, select block 11c, Hearing with a Veterans Law Judge. " Download VA Form 10182 PDF Learn more about requesting a Board Appeal How will I know when my hearing is scheduled? The Board of Veterans Appeals schedules hearings in docket order the order in which they receive requests. You ll get a notice in the mail at least 30 days before your hearing is scheduled. It will let you know the date and time of the hearing and where it s located. Prepare for your Board hearing Can I bring evidence to my hearing? Yes , you can bring new and relevant evidence to your hearing to share with the judge. You also have 90 days after the day of your hearing to submit new evidence for the judge s review. Please don t submit new evidence prior to your hearing. Who can help me prepare for my hearing? A representative who s trained in the appeals process can help you prepare for your hearing and for the conversation with the judge. Your representative must be a VA - accredited lawyer, claims agent, or someone from a Veterans Service Organization VSO. Get help preparing for your hearing Find out what to expect at your Board hearing What will happen at my hearing? To start , the judge will swear you in, asking you to take an oath that you ll tell the truth during the hearing. You and the judge will have a conversation. The judge will listen to your testimony and may ask you a few questions. Your representative , if you have one , may help you at the hearing. You ll then : Tell the judge why you think you qualify for the VA benefits in your appeal Answer any questions the judge has about your appeal Share any new evidence with the judge Hearings usually last 30 minutes. When will the judge make a decision on my appeal? Your hearing will be transcribed and added to your appeal file. You ll be asked if you d like a copy of the transcript for your personal records. The judge will review the hearing and the other evidence in your appeal file before making a decision. When the 90-day time period for submitting new evidence closes , your case will be placed on the docket for a decision by a judge. If you don t have new evidence to submit , you can waive the 90-day time period that s automatically added to your appeal. Some Veterans Service Organizations will ask for time to make additional arguments in support of your case, so contact your representative first if you re thinking of waiving this time period. Reschedule or withdraw your Board hearing request What if I need to reschedule my hearing? You ll need to send a written request to the Board of Veterans Appeals providing good cause to reschedule your hearing at least 2 weeks before your hearing. Include your name, the VA file number for your appeal, and the reason why you need a new hearing date. Send your written request to the Board. Use the address or fax number listed below If your hearing is less than 2 weeks away , you ll need to file a motion explaining why you need to reschedule. Examples of good cause for rescheduling would include if you, your representative, or a witness are sick or you had trouble getting records. A judge will review your request , and you ll receive a notice in the mail when the judge decides if the hearing can be rescheduled. Will I lose my place in line if I reschedule my hearing? You won t lose your place in line. The Board of Veterans Appeals schedules hearings in docket order the order in which they receive requests. You ll keep the same docket date , and your hearing will be rescheduled to the earliest date possible. What if I need to withdraw my hearing? If you change your mind about having a hearing , you can write to the Board to withdraw cancel your hearing request. This won t speed up your appeal unless you also switch to the Direct Review appeal option, which can only be done at certain times. If you switch to a different appeal option , you ll keep the same docket date and your appeal will be decided at the same time as other appeals with the same date. However , the time it takes to get a decision is different on each docket. If you need to withdraw your hearing request , you or your representative should send a written request at least 2 weeks before your hearing. Include your name, the VA file number for your appeal, and the reason you re withdrawing the appeal. You still have the option to submit new evidence, as long as you send it to the Board within 90 days of withdrawing your hearing request. Send your written request to the Board using the address or fax number listed below. Where do I send the Board a written request or new evidence? You can mail or fax a written request to reschedule or withdraw your Board hearing. You can also submit new evidence for the judge to review within 90 days of the day of your hearing. By mail Board of Veterans AppealsPO Box 27063Washington , D.C. 20038 By fax Fax your request to 844 - 678 - 8979.

# Local Politics and Governance

Broadening Horizons: Georgia's Infrastructure Development Initiatives Infrastructure development is an essential element of society progress, and nowhere is this more evident than in Georgia, where an ambitious public works program is helping create a more connected and efficient society. Since September 2023, a series of large-scale initiatives has begun that have brought growth and prosperity to Georgia's landscape; here we examine some transformative infrastructure development projects taking place that are redefining it while local communities play a crucial role in shaping these ventures. Identification of Change Needs Georgia's development strategy places infrastructure rebuild at its heart. Local officials, led by Marietta Mayor Alice Johnson and her team, have made September 15, 2023, official as the start date of a comprehensive review process to identify areas needing improvement across Georgia's public works system. Transport was given particular emphasis during initial assessments. Traffic studies commissioned by Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) and conducted over the fall revealed significant congestion issues which not only delay travel times but also have negative repercussions for both people and the environment. Engaging Community Stakeholders Mayor Johnson acknowledged the value of citizen involvement by initiating a series of town hall meetings starting October 5, 2023 to invite community members to voice their concerns and offer suggestions. With "Your Voice, Your Georgia" serving as promotional materials' motto, citizens became active contributors towards creating an infrastructure blueprint. These town halls gave residents an opportunity to meet with representatives from GDOT, urban planners like Jeremy Linquist and environmental groups to discuss anticipated projects and their possible effects on everyday life. This transparent dialogue served as a means of reaching consensus while creating an appropriate development plan. Prioritizing Projects With Public Input From these community discussions, GDOT with help of expert civil engineers like Sandra Kim identified priority projects which aligned both with state strategic goals and public needs expressed by residents. Through collaboration, projects were then classified according to urgency, potential impact, and cost-effectiveness. One of the standout projects is the Atlanta East Corridor Expansion, which seeks to facilitate greater connectivity between suburban areas and the city center. First proposed by civic leader Tomas Herrera, this initiative quickly gained support within the community for its potential to reduce commute times while simultaneously sparking economic activity in less accessible neighborhoods. Securing Funding for Implementation Georgia's local governments, led by financial strategist Roberta Chu, have explored various financing solutions to bring projects to fruition. An infrastructure bill passed by the Georgia state legislature on November 18th 2023 provided significant funds for priority initiatives as well as creating an infrastructure bill public-private partnership framework designed to attract investments from business. Georgians also approved special infrastructure bonds and a temporary increase in local sales taxes through public referendum, showing their eagerness to invest in Georgia's future prosperity and also acting as an insurance policy against overly relying on any one source of revenue. Leveraging Technology and Innovation Georgia's infrastructure initiative relies heavily on innovative technology. At a press event held on December 1, 2023, plans for intelligent traffic systems and eco-friendly public transportation solutions were unveiled, reflecting Georgia's focus on innovation. Anita Patel's ambitious Smart Grid System plan promises to improve traffic flow and reduce emissions through real-time data analytics, ultimately relieving traffic issues while helping the state meet its sustainability targets. Coordination between regional and federal agencies. No large-scale infrastructure project can succeed by itself, and Georgia's initiatives are no different. One impressive example of federal and regional collaboration can be seen with their efforts for the Appalachian Highway Extension project; this interstate collaboration, led by Regional Director Franklin O'Connor of neighboring states and scheduled to begin construction sometime after 2024 will create an important transportation link that facilitates interstate commerce and travel. Community Resilience to Tackle Challenges Development projects of such scale invariably face numerous hurdles and difficulties, from logistical setbacks to public skepticism. Yet Georgia has consistently shown resilience through an engaged and informed community. Situations like the material shortage experienced in March 2024 were met not with frustration but instead with a collective spirit to find solutions. Hardin Construction provided alternative supplies while community forums discussed ways to keep projects moving without compromising quality or standards. Monitoring and Maintaining Momentum As these projects developed from 2024 on, keeping public interest and support intact was of utmost importance. Quarterly updates presented at community gatherings or online platforms kept everyone abreast of progress or setbacks. By creating an effective review mechanism, such as Commissioner Lisa Grant has ensured that these public works projects remain accountable to taxpayers. Additionally, this framework enables immediate adjustments to be made as construction and development dynamics change quickly and decisively. Nurturing Future Innovators and Leaders Georgia recognizes that to sustain long-term growth requires investing in its most essential resource - its people. Programs like Georgia Young Builders Initiative are being piloted in schools starting May 2025 in order to inspire and educate future architects, engineers and urban planners. Students take part in hands-on workshops led by accomplished professionals like architect Marco Duarte that enable them to understand the principles behind their communities' transformation, helping ensure that Florida's infrastructure legacy will be preserved by capable, visionary minds for decades to come. Foster a Culture of Continuous Improvement Success in infrastructure development is not a static state but an ongoing journey of improvement. Georgia has taken this philosophy to heart by actively revising and adapting plans as necessary, with feedback mechanisms and annual review sessions by the Public Works Council keeping long-term vision in step with current realities and emerging needs. Georgia's infrastructure development initiatives stand as a testament to the successful pairing of strategic planning, community involvement, and an unrelenting pursuit of excellence. Since 2023, dozens of projects have been initiated that aim at creating an enduring legacy - one which will lead Georgian communities toward brighter and more connected futures.
