# Local Politics and Governance

Campaign Finance Reform and its Impact on Local Elections In the quaint town hall of Eldridge, a modest crowd gathers on October 5, 2023, where city council candidates are discussing a matter that has increasingly influenced the tide of municipal elections: campaign finance reform. It is an issue that roots decades back in our political landscape, stirring passions and debates over the fairness and equitability of local electoral processes. The Dawning of Financial Awareness in Local Politics Going back in time, campaign finance scarcely made the headlines in local elections. During the 1970s and 1980s, the majority of city council and mayoral races operated on shoestring budgets, fueled by small donations, bake sales, and door-to-door canvassing. It was an era when candidates could run on character and policy rather than the depth of their coffers. A vintage photograph of a 1978 mayoral campaign in Eldridge still hangs in the community center, showcasing a hopeful candidate speaking to a small but rapt audience in the local park, his campaign budget likely not exceeding a few hundred dollars. The Advent of Big Money in Local Races As the years progressed, particularly as we moved through the late 1990s into the early 2000s, the dynamics in local election campaigns began to evolve, echoing the gradual corporatization of American politics. It became apparent that candidates with the most signage, the slickest mailers, and the most pervasive radio spots had a distinct advantage. This invariably led to a growing sentiment that local offices were drifting away from community grassroots and inching towards the highest bidder – or the candidate with the heftiest war chest. In Eldridge, that change was felt in April 1997 when mayoral candidate Jonathan Smith raised a staggering $50,000 for his campaign, dwarfing the funds of his opponents and securing a victory that left many pondering the implications for future races. The resonance of money, it seemed, had infiltrated the once humble forums of municipal governance. The Tug-of-War Over Donations and Spending By 2004, activists and concerned citizens began calling for increased transparency and limitations in campaign finance. The idea was not radical but rooted in a nostalgia for smalltown democracy where one's voice and vote seemed to carry more weight than the thickness of a candidate's wallet. Debates and ballot measures involving campaign spending limits, donor disclosure requirements, and even public financing of campaigns started to emerge with recurring intensity. Consider the case of Eldridge's neighboring town, Wellsville, where in 2009, Christina Martinez, a retired school teacher, shocked the local political stage by running a campaign solely on micro-donations, averaging $20, and a stringent self-imposed spending cap. The election was a watershed moment, suggesting that perhaps the pendulum could swing back toward a more equitable battleground. The Fight for Campaign Finance Reform It wasn't until September 16, 2015, that Eldridge experienced a game-changing moment in its handling of campaign finances. The catalyst was none other than the city council candidate Carmen Reynolds, a former journalist and reform advocate who introduced a comprehensive campaign finance reform package. The ‘Reynolds Reform’, as it would later be known, aimed to not only limit the amount of money candidates could raise but also where they could source their funds from, putting a cap on both individual and corporate contributions. After a heated debate period, replete with challenges from established political figures and pushback from local business entities, the Reynolds Reform was passed. Critics warned of unintended consequences and barriers to newcomer candidates, while proponents praised the new legislation as a resurrection of true electoral competition. The Face of Post-Reform Local Politics Post-reform, the election cycles of the late 2010s and early 2020s witnessed a diversification in the profiles of those who ran for local offices. Grassroots candidates, once overshadowed by the financial prowess of their rivals, found themselves on a more level playing field. The elections in these recent years have had a different feel, a throwback to the intimate community-involved politics of the past. Town halls have become vibrant with debate again, not merely platforms for the most well-funded campaigns to parade their polished messages. Eldridge saw its most reflective transformation during the 2021 mayoral race. Amy Li, a long-time local librarian and community organizer, ascended to the office with a stunningly low campaign budget of just under $10,000. Her door-to-door campaign and reliance on social media for outreach were a testament to the impact of finance reforms in leveling the playing field. The Current Echoes and Future of Finance Reforms As we sit in the community hall watching the city council candidates share their visions for the future, it's not lost on the Eldridge electorate how these campaign finance reforms have changed the contours of local governance. Whether it’s the decreased financial barrier for entry, the increased transparency, or the restored sense of democratically-powered candidacy, the impact has been profound. Today, the issue of campaign finance in local elections remains far from settled. While some argue that further restrictions are needed to prevent external influences and to maintain the purity of local elections, others advocate for relaxing certain reforms, warning against overly stringent regulations that might stifle political engagement and creative campaigning. As we navigate the complexities of our political environment and reflect on the financial journey our local elections have taken over the decades, one statement rings out during the meeting that captures the essence of our collective nostalgia: “It's about bringing power back to the people of Eldridge, where it belongs.” The debate over the future of campaign finance in local elections is indeed alive and well in Eldridge – a testament to the enduring village hall democracies that remain the bedrock of our political system. The legacy of finance reform carries with it the promise of a more equitable electoral playground and the hope for a continued tradition of community-driven leadership. It is a hope that as the leaves turn this election season, everyone in Eldridge feels within reach.

# Local Environmental Issues

Restoring the Wild: Safeguarding Our Local Biodiversity In an era where urban sprawl and industrial advancement continue to encroach upon natural spaces, the necessity for targeted wildlife habitat conservation strategies has never before been so urgent. As bustling cities expand and climates shift, the plight of local wildlife becomes increasingly dire, with many species teetering on the brink of extinction. Such is the state of our local ecosystems, where immediate action is not only ideal but critical for the survival of numerous species that call our region home. The Plight of Endangered Species Since September 1, 2023, reports by environmental conservation groups like the Green Shield Initiative have indicated a sharp decline in native species population. This decline is attributed primarily to habitat loss, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. As these threats persist, the scenarios facing creatures such as the painted turtle, the spotted owl, and the various native pollinators remain bleak. Dr. Emily Waters, a renowned ecologist and advocate for local habitat preservation, emphasizes the urgency: "We're at a critical juncture where, without immediate intervention, we risk losing not just wildlife, but the integrity of our entire ecosystem." The call for immediate action is propelled by the fact that the loss of even a single species can disrupt the balance of local food webs, leading to unforeseen consequences for biodiversity and ecosystem health. Implementing Conservation Strategies At the heart of the issue is the question: What can be done? Conservation strategies are as diverse as the ecosystems they aim to protect, ranging from legal protections to grassroots community activism. Here, we delve into some of the most promising approaches being adopted in our local communities. Protected Areas and Legal Measures A primary strategy has been the establishment of protected areas where development is restricted and wildlife can thrive without human interference. Since October 2023, three new wildlife preserves have been founded in the region, providing critical refuge for many endangered species. Legal measures also play an instrumental role in protecting vulnerable populations. Councilwoman Linda Zhang, who spearheaded the latest biodiversity bill passed on December 12, 2023, states, "Strengthening our environmental legislation not only deters harmful activities but also sets a precedent for the protection of our natural heritage." Habitat Restoration Initiatives Another promising avenue is habitat restoration. By rehabilitating ecosystems that have been degraded, conservationists help to restore the natural environment to a state where native species can once again flourish. Projects range from replanting native flora to reshaping rivers to favor natural flow and support aquatic life. On February 22, 2024, the Green Helmets, a volunteer group, successfully restored a 50-acre wetland that had previously been drained for agriculture. This effort alone has seen the return of several amphibian species previously thought to have vanished from the area. Education and Community Engagement Engagement with the community is essential. Conservation is not a task for a few dedicated individuals but a collective responsibility. Educational programs designed to foster respect and understanding for local wildlife have been effective. For example, the Wild Wisdom Workshop, initiated on March 15, 2024, reached over 5,000 students, inspiring young minds about the importance of habitat conservation and sparking interest in environmental stewardship. Local involvement doesn't stop at education. Community-led projects, like the "Backyard Sanctuaries" initiative launched on April 5, 2024, encourage citizens to create wildlifefriendly spaces in their own gardens. Michael Johnson, a homeowner who transformed his lawn into a vibrant habitat says, "It's empowering to see how small, individual actions can collectively make a significant impact on the well-being of our local species." Collaboration with Industry and Agriculture The willingness to work cooperatively with industries and agricultural entities is paramount for successful conservation. Strategies that include the adoption of environmentally-friendly practices — such as the reduction of pesticide use and the creation of ecological corridors through farmlands — are gaining momentum. Samantha Lee, CEO of AgroTech Farming Solutions, explained on June 30, 2024, how transitioning to conservation agriculture not only safeguards habitats but also enhances soil health and sustainability for farmers. Invasive Species Control As non-native species continue to displace local wildlife, strategies to manage these invasive populations are critical. Initiatives underway include the Community Predator Watch program that began on August 10, 2024, which trains citizens to identify and report sightings of invasive predators so that swift action can be taken to remove these threats from sensitive habitats. Adaptive Management and Monitoring To ensure that conservation strategies remain effective, adaptive management is necessary. This process involves the continuous monitoring of ecosystems and population trends, followed by adjustments to conservation methods as new information becomes available. For instance, the Longleaf Pine Restoration project ongoing since September 2024 has been adjusting its planting techniques based on the survival rates and growth patterns observed in newly rehabilitated areas. Funding and Resource Allocation Funding is the lifeblood of conservation efforts. Campaigns to secure public and private funding for habitat conservation have been ramping up, with the recent "Dollars for Diversity" fundraiser accruing over $2 million for local conservation projects as of October 5, 2024. Efficient allocation of these funds to high-impact projects is crucial, as is the auditing to ensure that money is being used effectively to safeguard our wildlife heritage. Climate Change Mitigation Finally, no modern conservation strategy would be complete without addressing the overarching issue of climate change. Local initiatives have focused on reducing carbon emissions through community tree-planting events and advocating for renewable energy sources in municipal energy plans. Such actions not only contribute to global efforts to mitigate climate change but also directly benefit local habitats by contributing to a stable climate that supports diverse species. In conclusion, conservation strategies are only as good as the commitment shown by each stakeholder in the community. It is critical to transform the urgency into continuous, concerted action from individuals, businesses, government, and non-profits alike. As we advance, it's imperative that we recognize the interconnected nature of society and the natural world, fostering a harmonious coexistence that ensures the survival and prosperity of both local wildlife and human communities. Only through immediate and sustained action can we hope to restore and conserve the precious habitats that are vital to our region's ecological health.

# News Stories

Hailstorm Tales from Cairo: Unusual Weather Wreaks Havoc Cairo residents were awakened Monday morning to what some may describe as an apocalyptic scene: their city had been covered with snow since an unlikely hailstorm struck early that morning, leaving an expanse of white debris on its streets and blanketing much of Cairo with whiteness. Cairo is known for its rich historical legacy; rarely do residents witness such chilling weather phenomena as Cairo recently experienced during an unprecedented hailstorm caused by sudden shift in high altitude winds that brought an icy front from the Mediterranean seashore. Egypt Meteorological Authority reported the hailstorm as "extreme and unprecedented". Aftermath The aftermath was startling to say the least; iconic palm trees lay bent and broken, ancient walkways covered in ice, and once-busy streets came to a sudden standstill. Local shop owner Ahmed Al-Masri recounted, "This hailstorm felt as though the sky were opening up - no one was prepared!" Children, often on their way to school at this hour, could be seen playing among the snowstrewn landscape, their laughter in stark contrast to their parents' worried facial expressions. For an otherwise warm population used to shielding itself from sunlight and not from frozen pellets from above, this day was unprecedented. Infrastructure Under Threat of Collapse By midday, reports of damage began pouring in. Cairo's infrastructure, designed to withstand intense sunlight and occasional sandstorms, had been severely compromised by hail's rapid accumulation, leading to collapsed flat roofs as a result of their weight. This caused structural damages across various structures both old and new. Traffic came to a virtual halt as vehicles, unaccustomed to such treacherous road conditions, skidded and collided resulting in hundreds of minor accidents across Cairo. Emergency services struggled to respond to the thousands of calls for help made at once - Hanaa Mostafa, a paramedic from Cairo stated that her ambulances "could barely navigate through icy streets - every call for assistance became a fight against nature". Rural Areas Are No Safe Haven Egypt's rural outskirts were hit hard by the storm's fury, with agriculture and its central role in Egypt's economy and food security particularly hard hit by hailstones pelting fields of crops; Mahmoud Abbas of Cairo Farm may lose all his season's yield due to hailstone damage. Abbas, an experienced date farmer for life, said "The impact was devastating; my orchard has been decimated and we can only hope some crops can be saved, especially at this crucial stage of harvesting." "This came out of nowhere and left my entire orchard devastated; our harvest schedule could hardly have been worse." Impacts on Ancient Monuments Cairo, an epicenter of rich culture and ancient history, boasts numerous iconic monuments that felt the brunt of Mother Nature's fury this summer. Giza Plateau, home to iconic structures like the Great Pyramids and Sphinx, was exposed to potential erosion risks; experts like Dr. Lana Fouad from Cairo University's Restoration and Conservation faculty highlighted their concerns. "Though these structures have stood the test of time, extreme weather may still damage them: hailstorms can speed up erosion both stone and mortar alike," explained Fouad. Although initial assessments revealed no major damage, the hailstorm has spurred calls for a review on how best to safeguard these ancient marvels in light of climate change's increasingly harsh weather extremes. Public Services Are Being Strapped Down City services worldwide serve as the frontline responders in times of natural calamities; Cairo was no different, as emergency teams, sanitation departments, healthcare providers and city officials worked around-the-clock to manage effects from hailstorm. "We are doing all we can to clear the streets and ensure everyone's safety," stated Khaled Nassar of Cairo's Urban Search and Rescue team, adding, "Cairo is strong; its people are resilient; together we will overcome this." Climate-Related Issues and Concerns in California Due to Cairo's unseasonal hailstorm, environmental scientists like Dr. Samir Atta of Ain Shams University are raising the alarm on climate action being taken urgently. Dr. Atta suggested, "While individual events cannot definitively be linked to climate change, patterns we're witnessing suggest we're departing from normal weather patterns. Therefore, it's time that we tackle their causes and prepare more effectively for their consequences." The International Eye Images and videos of Cairo's hailstorm went viral across social media. International aid agencies, environmental activists, and foreign governments issued statements of support and offered assistance following its devastating aftermath. Cairo's hailstorm serves as a powerful reminder of Earth's rapidly shifting climate dynamics and highlights the urgent need for global resiliency and cooperation to maintain sustainable weather patterns. Furthermore, it illustrates just how susceptible even seemingly isolated regions can be to sudden weather shifts that threaten survival. At nightfall over Cairo's heart, memories of this surreal day began to dissipate; yet its impact will undoubtedly stay with its residents for some time to come. Whether this event sparks local and international initiatives to address climate change remains to be seen; regardless, Cairo will rise up to meet the challenges associated with rebuilding, recovering, and planning for an uncertain climatic future in coming days.

# Local Environmental Issues

Urban Air Quality and Public Health Risks: A Global Crisis In the burgeoning metropolises of our contemporary world, where the skyline is oft adorned by the shimmering facades of progress, a less visible yet insidious entity looms— air pollution. As of September 2, 2023, according to a study released by the World Health Organization, 91% of the world’s population resides in areas where air quality levels surpass the limits deemed safe for human health. This alarming statistic casts a shadow over urban life, revealing a public health crisis that urgently beckons a call to action. The Grim Portrait of Urban Atmosphere The conundrum of urban air quality is a multifaceted issue influenced by industrial emissions, vehicular exhalations, and energy production. Often trapped by the very architecture that houses these activities, pollutants such as nitrogen oxides, particulate matter, and volatile organic compounds envelop cities in a haze that is both literal and figurative. Public health risks associated with poor air quality are manifold—ranging from respiratory conditions like asthma and bronchitis to cardiovascular diseases and strokes. A recent report on October 10, 2023, outlined that urban air pollution accounts for an estimated 4.2 million premature deaths worldwide every year. Dr. Emily Chan, a leading epidemiologist, emphasizes that "air pollution is not merely an inconvenience; it is a significant threat to public health, comparable to global epidemics." A City’s Breathing Lessons: Mitigation Efforts Cities worldwide are taking strides to mitigate the adverse effects of air pollution on health. For instance, on December 5, 2023, Paris implemented an ambitious program banning diesel vehicles manufactured before 2001 from the city center. Such policy-driven approaches aim to curtail vehicular emissions—one of the primary culprits of urban air pollution. Similarly, in Tokyo, a city often hailed for its clean air despite its dense population, stringent regulations and the widespread adoption of cutting-edge technology in transport and industry have made significant strides in air quality improvement. Lessons from Tokyo suggest that a combination of technological innovation and rigorous oversight can lead to heightened urban air standards. The Tale of Two Cities: Contrasting Fortunes A comparative look at Beijing and Copenhagen showcases two disparate narratives within the urban air quality discourse. Where Beijing wrestles with the tenebrous smog resulting from coal dependence and rapid industrialization, Copenhagen basks in the veneration of its bicycle-friendly avenues and renewable energy infrastructure, which has greatly reduced air pollution levels. The measures adopted by the Danish capital, such as widespread cycling lanes and the promotion of electric vehicles, underscore the crucial role of sustainable urban planning in combating air pollution. In contrast, Beijing has seen intermittent success with measures like traffic restrictions and factory shutdowns during severe smog episodes but faces an uphill battle in realizing long-term air quality improvements. Pioneering Clean Air Innovations In the technological arena, innovative strategies are being pioneered to remedy the malaise of urban air pollution. On September 15, 2023, the city of Rotterdam unveiled the world's first smog vacuum cleaner, using patented ion technology to cleanse the air effectively. Meanwhile, green infrastructure projects, such as the vertical gardens of Milan, integrate plant life into urban layouts, which not only beautify the environment but also actively filter air pollutants. The adoption of such green innovations, while of high initial cost, posits profound implications for urban spaces. By prioritizing clean air technologies and infrastructure, cities invest in the health and well-being of their inhabitants, laying the groundwork for a sustainable urban legacy. Citizen Response: The Power of Public Mobilization Public awareness and activism have burgeoned in response to the urban air quality crisis. Citizen-led initiatives like the 'Clean Air for My Child' campaign, started by a group of concerned parents in London on November 23, 2023, have spurred governmental action and raised crucial public discourse on the issue of air pollution. As advocate James Sullivan underscores, “when the people demand change, change becomes inevitable.” Public health insights reveal that knowledge dissemination and community engagement are potent forces in driving policy changes. By equipping citizens with the tools to understand and challenge the sources of urban air pollution, cities can leverage collective responsibility to foster healthier environments. The Role of International Cooperation The International Coalition for Clean Air, established on January 21, 2024, embodies the spirit of cross-border collaboration essential in addressing the global challenge of urban air pollution. By sharing research, best practices, and technology, countries can enhance their individual and collective capacity to improve urban air quality. A Forward Gaze: Next Steps in the Battle for Clean Air As highlighted by the annual Urban Air Quality Summit held in San Francisco on February 28, 2024, the endeavor to transcend the air quality impasse is fraught with complexities, requiring a cohesive blend of technology, policy, and public engagement. The implementation of clean air zones, investment in public transportation, and stringent emissions standards remain pivotal to the crusade against air pollution. The intertwining of urban air quality and public health is a narrative that continues to unfold. The need for vigilant, sustained, and collaborative effort to improve the air we breathe is an integral part of the quest for healthier, more sustainable urban living. As urban landscapes burgeon and morph, it is incumbent upon us—as global citizens, policymakers, and stewards of the environment—to seek and impart solutions that ensure the air, that most basic medium of life, remains a source of sustenance and not of harm. Cities across the world must take heed of the lessons that can be learned from each other, with the clear objective of weaving those lessons into the fabric of a clear-sky future.

# Regional Folklore and Myths

The Tale of the Vanishing Fisherman: An Eerie Echo in Coastal Lore On an ordinary morning in the quaint coastal town of Willow's End, the ocean whispered an ancient story to those who dared listen. It was October 14, 2023, when the town woke to the mysterious disappearance of an experienced fisherman, Peter McLeary. Yet, the locals knew that Peter's story was not a singular event. It resonated with a tale that had been handed down through generations—a tale that encapsulated the community's deep respect for the sea, and which has shaped their environmental conscience. This is the tale of the Vanishing Fisherman, an enigmatic legend that continues to influence local fishing practices and ocean conservation efforts. An ECHO FROM THE PAST For centuries, coastal communities have depended on the sea for their livelihood. Yet, they have also been home to many legends that speak of the sea's mighty power. The Tale of the Vanishing Fisherman could be traced back to a blustery night on September 17, 1819, when local fisherman, John Haddock, went out to sea and never returned. As the story goes, John had often boasted about his confrontational approach to fishing. Ignoring the long-standing community beliefs about respecting the ocean's bounty, he took more than his share. The tale purports that, as punishment for his greed, the sea reclaimed John Haddock, leaving no trace of his boat or his body. The story has stood as a solemn warning to the fishers of Willow's End. It carries a wisdom about the delicate balance required in fishing practices—a respect for the sea and understanding that its resources are not infinite. AN UNSETTLING PRESENT Fast forward to present-day Willow's End, and we find that the ocean has not lost its capacity for both giving and taking away. Peter McLeary was no John Haddock; he was known for his sustainable practices and advocacy for ocean conservation. However, his sudden disappearance under calm weather conditions revived the old legend amongst the townsfolk, igniting a visceral reminder of nature's unpredictability and the ever-present need for sustainable interaction with our environment. What happened to Peter McLeary remains a mystery, but his disappearance has acted as a catalyst for the community to reflect on their relationship with the sea. It has reinvigorated discussion around how traditional lore and contemporary environmental concerns intersect and reaffirm the importance of protecting the ocean. THE SEA'S WARNING Even with modern advancements and scientific approaches to fishing, the Tale of the Vanishing Fisherman reminds us of the age-old wisdom of moderation. The sea, they say, watches and remembers. And though it offers nourishment, it demands respect. The story serves as a parable, cautioning against overfishing and urging for harmony between mankind and the marine environment. This local legend has become more than a spine-chilling story told around the fire. It's a narrative woven into the fabric of Willow's End's fishing policies. It shapes local regulations that prevent overfishing and promote marine conservation, ensuring that fish stocks are allowed to replenish, and that the marine ecosystem is maintained for future generations. FROM LEGEND TO LESSONS It's crucial to note that the story is more than a scare tactic; it's education through folklore. Parents tell it to their children, and teachers to their students, as a lesson in sustainability. The legend has inspired initiatives such as the Willow's End Annual Ocean Festival, held since 2023, to educate citizens on responsible fishing practices and the significance of ocean conservation. Regional programs developed in light of this legend include 'Net Gains', a community-led effort that encourages local fishers to adopt practices that mitigate bycatch and preserve juvenile fish populations. 'Seas of Tomorrow', another local project initiated on April 22, 2024, works to restore coral reefs and marine habitats, acknowledging that sustainable fishing isn't just about the fish—it's about caring for their environment. A COMMUNITY UNITED Perhaps the most heartwarming aspect of this tale's unfolding is the unity it fosters within the community. Annual remembrance ceremonies for those lost at sea, like Peter McLeary, have become a linchpin for community solidarity over conservation efforts. Funds raised during these ceremonies go towards marine research and rescue operations, ensuring that while the town remembers its past, it's also looking toward the future. The seaside vigils, where townsfolk gather to light lanterns and set them adrift on the waves, are a sight to behold. They reflect the collective spirit—a community standing as the guardian of the ocean. The legend of the Vanishing Fisherman doesn't divide the people of Willow's End; it unites them in a shared responsibility to protect the planet's most vital resource. BRIDGING MYTHS AND MODERNITY In retaining the essence of this tale, Willow's End exemplifies how folklore can seamlessly integrate with contemporary conservation efforts. The narrative continues to evolve, a legend that breathes life into environmental advocacy without losing its traditional allure. While skeptics might see the story as mere superstition, those invested in environmental protection recognize its value. The tale has proven to be an effective vessel for passing down conservation ethics from one generation to the next. REFLECTIONS WITHOUT CONCLUSIONS Every legend leaves room for interpretation, and The Tale of the Vanishing Fisherman is no exception. It's a story without end, much like the ongoing effort to understand and steward the natural world. Each telling of the tale is an opportunity to reflect on the dynamic relationship humans have with the sea. As the town of Willow's End carries on the legacy of Peter McLeary, his story intertwines with centuries-old folklore to reaffirm an unwavering commitment to ocean conservation. The Vanishing Fisherman is more than just a ghostly fable; it's a continuous reflection of a community's dedication to living in balance with the forces of nature—an echo from the depths that guides the way forward. In the restless whispers of the waves, one can still hear the cautionary echoes of the past, and in the hearts of the townspeople, a resolve for a sustainable future beats strong. The tale lives on, and so does the mission it represents; the ocean's bounty is to be revered, its mysteries acknowledged, and its future, unequivocally, to be preserved by the hands that fish its waters.

Totally disabled or terminally ill policyholders If you have VA life insurance coverage and you become totally disabled or terminally ill , you may be eligible for certain benefit options. Your spouse may also be eligible for certain options if diagnosed with a terminal illness. Learn more about these options and how to apply. On this page SGLI extension for policyholders who become disabled Waiver of premiums for policyholders who become totally disabled Accelerated benefits for terminally ill policyholders SGLI extension for policyholders who become disabled Can I get a free extension of my SGLI coverage if I m disabled when I leave the military? You may be able to keep your coverage for up to 2 years after the date you left the military if you meet both of the requirements listed below. Both of these must be true. You re: Within 2 years of your separation date , and Totally disabled or have certain conditions Find out if you qualify for the SGLI Disability Extension and how to apply Waiver of premiums for policyholders who become totally disabled A waiver of premiums means you won t have to pay your life insurance premiums the monthly payments you make to hold your policy. In most cases , premiums can only be waived up to one year prior to receipt of a claim. You may be able to get a waiver of your life insurance premiums if you meet all of the requirements listed below. All of these must be true : You have a mental or physical disability that prevents you from being able to hold a job , and You re covered under Service - Disabled Veterans Life Insurance S - DVI , and Your total disability happens before you re 65 years old but after the effective date of your life insurance policy , and Your total disability continues for at least 6 months in a row There are certain exceptions to the above conditions. But if you think you re entitled to a waiver of premiums , you should apply as soon as possible and we ll let you know if you qualify. You ll need to apply for these benefits as soon as possible after becoming totally disabled. We ll decide if you qualify , and we ll notify you of the decision. To apply , fill out and sign a Claim for Disability Insurance VA Form 29 - 357. Mail it to the address listed on the form. Download VA Form 29 - 357 PDF Accelerated benefits for terminally ill policyholders Eligible Veterans, service members, and covered spouses of service members can get accelerated benefits. With accelerated benefits , you can get up to 50% of the face value of your coverage in increments of $5,000 paid to you before death. You or your spouse may be able to get benefits early if you meet both of the requirements listed below. Both of these must be true : You have SGLI, Family SGLI, or VGLI life insurance, and You or your covered spouse has a written statement from a doctor saying you have or your spouse has 9 months or less to live Note : Only the insured service member or Veteran may apply for accelerated benefits. No one may apply on their behalf. In the case of a terminally ill spouse , only the insured may apply. Only the insured service member or Veteran may apply for accelerated benefits. No one may apply on their behalf. In the case of a terminally ill spouse , only the insured may apply. Use the application form for the type of coverage you have : If you re an SGLI or VGLI policyholder , fill out a Claim for Accelerated Benefits SGLV 8284. Download the Claim for Accelerated Benefits form If you re still on active duty or you re a Reservist , turn in the form to your service branch. They will need to fill out part of the form. If you re a Veteran , have your doctor fill out their part of the form, then send the completed form to : The Prudential Insurance Company of America PO Box 70173 Philadelphia , PA 19176 - 0173 Or , fax the form to 877 - 832 - 4943. If you re covered under Family SGLI , fill out a Claim for Accelerated Benefits SGLV 8284A. Download the Claim for Accelerated Benefits form You ll need to fill out one part of the application , and your doctor will need to fill out another part of it. We pay the remaining amount of the face value of the insurance policy to your designated beneficiary or beneficiaries the people you ve picked to get the money from your policy upon your death. In the case of a terminally ill spouse , we pay the rest of the insurance policy to you upon your spouse s death.
